Hey, welcome to the module on tonality, the secret weapon of influence. Now in this module, I'm going to reveal the single component that when mastered will set you apart from virtually every salesperson on the planet. You see the bottom line is this, that all things being equal, similar products, similar time frame that prospects will buy from people they like versus from people they don't like. Now I'm not saying that if you have a crappy offer or they don't need your product, people will buy from you because they're your friend. What I'm saying is that if you have a product that's great and they need it and the price is right, the offer makes sense, and someone else has virtually that identical offer and product, if they like you more than the other they're going to buy from you. The key is, and what so few salespeople realize is how instantly that connection happens, that likability so to speak, where someone either gets into a poll with you right there on the spot and just sends something about you that, wow, this is someone I want to do business with or not. They just say, nah, this is not someone that I want to listen to or just someone that doesn't connect with me on any level whatsoever and they tune you out. In fact, I tell that story that day in the brokerage room when I invented the straight line, I had those 12 brokers in front of me, I actually turned and drew on this board and I drew that straight line for the first time and I said, listen guys, if you don't capture these people's attention in literally four seconds flat, you're done. If they don't think that you have certain very critical elements right from the beginning, bam, they will tune out and not listen to you. Now this goes far beyond anything you ever learned about before about building rapport or establishing trust. This is about identifying those intangible elements of communication, that tonality, that subtle tonality and then lining them up and move them both emotionally and logically so your prospect has that unconscious and conscious connection with you and because of that, they want to do business with you and it's also about how to say things in a certain way that compels people to take immediate action. Let me back up for a second here and start by defining what rapport actually is and what it really means is this, it's to capture and keep your prospect's attention and get into, genuinely connect with you and to like you on both a conscious and an unconscious level. Now from the behavioral aspect here, what rapport is, it's how to use a combination of your words, your tonality and your actual physiology, meaning how you move your body to create a sense of alignment with another human being. Either you get into rapport with someone and have the ability to influence them, connect, or you are not in rapport and it's virtually impossible to influence them. Rapport on the deepest, most fundamental level, it's about two things. Number one is I care. I care about you. I'm not here to just make a commission and rip your eyeballs out. I actually care about you. If someone thinks that you care, it goes a long way to getting into rapport. The second element is just like you and as human beings, we will associate based on commonalities. We don't associate based on our differences. If I was going to go join a club, just imagine if you and I were married, Victoria, and I came home and said, hey honey, I just joined this amazing club. There's not a person there like me. I'm a Republican, they're Democrats. They're all dentists. I'm a construction worker. They hate playing golf. I love golf. I joined. You'd say, what's wrong with you? I had nothing in common with them. But if I said to you, hey, Vic, just got a great club. They're Democrats. They all love to do outdoor work. They all love playing golf. They all the same religious beliefs. I joined. You say, oh, you say, well, how much did it cost? But you might say to me, oh, go, at least make sense to you. We associate, we connect based on similarities. So this idea of getting into a poor is partially about likeability, finding common ground.
